sentences.text: [TONE]
You're welcome.
I was born in Satu Mare.
At that time, it was Romania.
And the year was 1926, June 12.
No.
At the time was Cathy, Catherine Lebi.
Cathy.
Yes.
Well, that area, at that time, it belonged to Romania.
The whole region is called Transylvania.
I'm sure you heard about it in connection with Dracula, which shouldn't be, but that's it.
It was a small town at that time, approximately 60,000, 70,000 people.
Well, the language, of course, the official language, it was Romanian, because it belonged to Romania.
We spoke Hungarian at home.
My native language was Hungarian.
And, of course, in school, we learned in Romanian.
That was normal.
And what should I say?
I belonged to a middle-class family.
My father was a merchant selling lumber.
What was his name?
His name was Henry Lebi-- L-E-B-I.
My mother's name was Blanka, but here it would be Blanche.
But at that time, that was Blanka.
I was an only child.
Of course, I was so spoiled.
I think that's the right expression.
You could say that.
Sheltered, but really spoiled.
How did we live?
It was a small town.
Of course, almost-- most people knew each other.
We lived in a nice residential area in a nice house.
We had a big garden.
What percentage of the population was Jewish in your town?
Well, that was a big percentage.
I would say there were about 60,000 people.
There were about 6,000 Jews, so I should say 10%.
Well, the majority, the bigger part of the Jewish population, were Orthodox.
We were in the minority, because we were, what they call here Reform.
We were that kind.
Well, I wouldn't say that this was a problem, but we were really not too much connected.
How should I say?
We belonged to another area.
They belonged to another area.
And interest was different, and the view, and observance of religion, and so on.
It was different, yes.
Oh, yes.
Actually, my parents had a kosher house until 1943.
And we observed the holidays, yes, the big holidays, like the Yom Kippur, the Rosh Hashanah, and even the Passover.
That's it.
My parents didn't go to the Temple on Fridays and Saturdays.
But I did for awhile.
No, just high school, my father.
And my mother had just junior high school.
She went to school only until she was 16, junior high school and then two more years just general education.
My father had high school.
Oh, what do I remember?
Everything was wonderful.
I had a very good life.
They loved me.
Maybe they loved too much.
What did I do?
Of course, I went to School, first to Jewish elementary school and then to convent junior high school.
And that was a very strict school.
Of course, we had to wear uniforms.
We had to wear ponytails and black stockings.
And we were not allowed to go to movies, but we still did do that, of course, mostly during the vacation.
What else?
Of course, I took private lessons, like most of my friends, of French, German, very little English.
Of course, we took private music lessons, like piano, violin.
I started with piano, but I was not too talented, and I was terribly lazy too, so after then they tried with the violin.
That went a little bit better, but not too good.
Yes, I would say.
There was theater.
My parents went to the theater quite often.
And, of course, there was movie, and so on.
Otherwise, well, where have we been, around the school, I guess, all right.
So you want me to continue that part?
That's what I wanted to come to, yes.
Well, at that time, of course, we heard the expression, anti-Semitism and discrimination and hatred and all these.
Personally, I did not experience any of those.
Naturally, we knew that it's not too good to be Jewish.
It-- it's not really exactly the same like the rest of the population, though, as I told you, personally, I did not feel that at that time.
Until 1940.
At 1940, I was 14 years old.
And then it was a big change, because the area where I lived became Hungary.
Hungary was a Fascist country with all the Fascist laws.
And when it became Hungary, right away the Fascist laws came into effect.
The first thing what affected me personally, it was that Jewish kids were not allowed to go to public school, only a very limited number.
Like in our city, all together about nine Jewish kids were allowed to continue education in public school.
You will ask me what was the criteria?
I haven't the slightest idea.
Of course, you had to be a very good student.
But I guess you had to be, first of all, lucky.
And probably, your parents had to have had some connections, I guess so.
In our city, I was among the lucky ones who was able to go to school.
What happened with the rest of them, they studied at home as well as they could.
And we all know that it's very, very difficult.
Very few parents were able financially to send the their kids in other cities where have been private Jewish schools, so that was hard.
That was really my first meeting with the meaning of the discrimination and anti-Semitist.
Then a few months later, they took away my father's business too, so we had some hardship, a little.

Well, I remember that my parents were very worried, because they knew that Hungary is a Fascist country, so they knew if we shall become Hungary, then it will be different than it was before when it belonged to Romania.
They were very concerned.
Of course, the war was going on.
I remember my parents listening to the radio all the time and to the British news also, what we could hear also.
And they were concerned what's going to happen with the world, what's going to happen with us.
But we didn't think of what is going to really happen, because nobody could predict that what it's going to be.
Well, so basically, our life was almost the same, only more problems and more hardship and more feeling the anti-Semitic things, what went on all around.
But my life, basically, was the same, still school, still movies and going to the parks and having fun, games, the kids, homework, and so on.
Still, I continued the lessons, private lessons also, the French, German.
Still I had the music lessons, private lessons.
Still I had the gym lessons.
So still my parents could do that for me.
I think it was a big sacrifice, but they did it.
Yes.
After his business was taken away, he just had some-- it was the stock market, the little things playing.
And they had some investments.
We had some properties, apartment house.
And the business, that was a big area where he had the office, a big territory where it was the lumber yard, so it was leased.
And we lived on the rental income.
Well, good middle class, or how should I say?
Yes.
Right.
I remember as the years were passing, it was already 1942.
And I remember Mother, like many other mothers, going to the railroad station.
And occasionally she took me with her.
They took-- brought some food, some clothes to Polish Jewish refugees who tried to escape from Poland at that time, because already the ghettos started in Poland, as we knew before.
It was occupied in 1939 by Germany.
For them, in order to go somewhere, to a western country, they had to travel through our city.
And occasionally, Mother took me to the railroad station also with her.
And that was the first time that I met people who had to leave everything behind just trying to escape with their bare life.
Of course, as we know, we knew later on, most of them were not accepted in the western countries.
Most of them were sent back.
But some succeeded.
Well, years were passing.
It was already 1944.
I remember the German army came onto-- into our city too, like all over occupied Europe.
And then new regulations and new laws, every day something else, mostly affecting us.
First thing that it was, we had wear yellow stars here on the left side on our clothes.
And we were not allowed to go to public places anymore.
That meant no more parks or movies or anything like that.
Somehow we were allowed to go to school.
But you can imagine how embarrassing it was to go to school with the yellow star.
We walked to the school, everyone just staring at you like you would have come from another planet, and you were exactly the same person whom you were just one day before.

Well, I was sort of lucky, because two girls from my class, non-Jewish girls, Christian girls, they decided that sometimes they will come to our house so I can walk with them to the school so I will feel less embarrassed.
And that meant a lot.
We were already in the last year of high school.
Everyone was getting ready to the graduation, how it's going to be.
And, of course, we couldn't think too much about that, because we had other problems being Jew and not knowing what to expect.
Well, the newest thing, newest regulation, it was that they would start in the ghettos in our cities too.
This came out, the new law, end of April, 1944.
Well, I knew-- we knew that graduation will be in May, end of May.
And somehow we applied in school that maybe we can graduate earlier than the rest of the class.
And it was allowed.
So we graduated earlier than everyone else, the written test, the verbal exam.
It was end of April when we did that, because already May the 3rd, they took us into the ghetto.
The ghetto, at that time, it has an absolutely different meaning what we think a ghetto is today.
It was a part of a residential area in the city.
Of course, it was gated all around.
And all around the gates, all the time, heavily armed soldiers and police.
They picked us up to take us into the ghetto.
We didn't go by ourselves.
Well, I don't know the-- exactly how many streets, but it was not a big area, of course.
They picked us up May the 3rd, 1944.
They came, the police, the soldiers, to each Jewish house, and brought us in with actually horse carts, and so on.
Of course, that was Hungary.
That was the Fascist Hungarian police, gendarmes, actually.
And what we were allowed to take with us, each person was allowed to take one mattress.
We were allowed to take some clothes, some dishes, some food, and some-- I don't know how, but we were allowed to take the radio also.
So it was May the 3rd, 1944, when they took us into the ghetto.
And, of course, the people from that certain area, they were evacuated who lived there.
And they were moved into our residences.
Now you ask, how did we live in the ghetto?
Since it was a residential area, there were houses.
There were apartments, and so on.
Like where we lived, for example, that was a house.
The house had three rooms, one bathroom, one kitchen.
In each room, there were 10 to 12 people on our own mattresses on the floor.
Of course, there was no other way, no more room.
We were so crowded, 10 to 12 mattresses in each room on the floor, that whenever anybody had to get up, inadvertently, somehow who had-- he had or she had to step on the other person's mattress.
There was no other way.
And one bathroom for 30 to 36 people, of course, it was not enough.
We always had to stand in line.
And it was a miracle if one could take a shower.
It was even a big deal to wash up.
We had to stand in line.
Well, actually we were able to decide to-- in what house we could stay.
Of course, we had to tell to the authorities, the Jewish authorities of the ghetto, what we prefer.
We chose a house because that house belonged to the dentist, the doctor where we used to go with our teeth, and he lived in that certain area.
So we decided that it would be nice to go into that house, because we knew that house.
Actually he was the uncle of one of my good friends, and who was also a schoolmate of mine, and whose picture I showed you, and I can show you later on again.
So we lived in that house.
And well, one kitchen for all the mothers, for that little meager food what we had.
It was very, very difficult, very, very complicated.
But the only important matter at that time, it was that we were together.
The families were together.

Actually my friends, Agnes, whom I was talking about, who was my oldest friend, whom I knew since I was born and we were friends and classmates, schoolmates, they lived there in that place too, with her mother and father.
She was also an only child.
And, of course, Anne, because her uncle lived there in that house, Anne and her parents also in that house.
So we had actually friends who we were living together.
Of course.
Yes.
We are still friends.
We meet every week at least once.
We are friends all the time.
No.
Not one single word.
Of course, we had the radio.
We could listen to the news.
But the news were only what it was allowed to be in the news.
It was censorship at that time.
The news were censored so everyone could hear only what the government wanted to be heard.
We couldn't listen there already at the English stations to hear, no.
We didn't know that anyone was deported.
We knew that there were ghettos because we were in ghettos.
And after a while, we heard that we are going to be taken into working camps.
That was resettlement, so-called.
And we were told that even if we are going to be taken into working camp like many others, the families are going to remain together.
So the only thing really what we were hoping for and what it matters that the families just to remain together.
Well, we were in the ghetto for a while, I remember.
I remember that the gendarme, the police, occasionally took one of the fathers, family heads, to be beaten up so-- to find out wherever, whoever, whenever we did hide jewelry or some valuables.
I remember they didn't tell me when they took my father.
But I just saw one afternoon my father was in bed.
He was feeling very bad.
And then that's when I found out that he was beaten up too.
They bait-- they did beat the sole of the feet to question, interrogate.
Well, supposedly-- my father said he didn't say anything.
That was one day I remember that too.
No, no.
He did not want me to know.
But somehow Mother told me after, next day, I guess.
Well, in a few days he was better.
Well, because they actually came to houses to confiscate some things.
And, of course, I remember we gave some jewelry, but I think not everything.
As far as I found out later on, no, not everything, just something.
And, of course, we leaved everything in the house, so they took everything what was in the house later on.
So that was in the ghetto.
We brought our own food.
It-- it was not too much food, but it was sort of enough.
Yes.
Absolutely.
That was very important.
People got along very well.
As we were standing in line in front of the bathroom, but nobody made a big deal out of it.
Of course, all the mothers in that one kitchen to cook, it was not easy.
But, actually, we didn't make a deal of all those things.
The only thing it mattered at that time, just the families to remain together.
We thought that we shall be in the ghetto for a long time, but no.
Ghetto was a very temporary solution, situation at that time.
Like in our city, we were 18,000 Jews in the ghetto from our city, from the surrounding smaller cities, and from the surrounding villages.
So as the time was passing, it was already approximately May the 14 or 15 when the newest regulation, it was that we are going to be taken away, relocated, as you said, in a working camp.
And we were told that the families are going to be together.
There were three groups taken come from the ghetto, each time 6,000 people, to the railroad station.
This I thought started approximately, as I told you, May the 14, something like that.
So these were the deportations, the rounding up for the deportations?
Yes.
Yes.
No, we were not told where is the working camp, somewhere in a working camp, that's all, no name.
Nobody knew it.
I was in the last group taken from the ghetto.
That was May 31st, 1944.
We were in the last group.
After that, there was no more ghetto in my hometown Satu Mare, Szatmarnemeti, as they called it at that time.
Well, let's go there when they took us.
It was May 31st.
Yes, we were together in the ghetto all the time.
Yes.
May 31st, going to the railroad station, it was a beautiful day.
That's a four-season area.
It was beginning of the summer.
And like in most European cities, the railroad station was at the end of the town.
So in order to go there, we had to walk through the whole town.
All around us, like all the time, always, the heavily armed soldiers and police.
So as we walked through the city, we were allowed to take a little small luggage with us, one change of clothes and one little food.
We had to walk through the whole city, the population just looking at us like, again, we would have come from another planet.
And we had the yellow stars.
We had to walk through the city.
We arrived to the railroad station.
The cattle cars are already there, these open doors waiting for us.
In no time, we were pushed inside.
The doors closed.
We were so many people in one cattle car, really crowded, squeezed together.
We were sitting on the floor, what else, in a cattle car.
And some people were not even able to sit down.
They were just standing, squeezed together.
Of course, there was not a real window, because cattle cars wouldn't have window.
There was some little like a window, small little opening, so little light came in, but not too much.
Well, the train started, destination unknown.
Of course, there was -- we had no toilet facilities in a cattle car.
How could it be?
There were some pails, some things in corner, what was used as toilet.
Of course, it was not enough.
Of course, it was overflowing.
Of course, it was unbelievable.
During this trip, which lasted from May 31st until June the 3rd, three days, three nights, we got twice two pails of waters, and that was it.
No.
They opened the doors when they gave us the two pails of water.
That's it.
No.
No.
No.
No.
Absolutely not.
Well, it-- the morales were very low already in that terrible circumstances.
And we didn't hope too much.
We just hoped that wherever they take us, to a working camp, because that's what we thought, we go to a working camp, just somehow to remain together.
Finally, after three days, three nights, in the morning, around 5 o'clock, very early--
OK.
